description: Electronic Arts is a major interactive entertainment publisher built around sports franchises, simulation games, licensed IP, and live-service ecosystems across console, PC, and mobile.

overview.title: Business profile
paragraphs: Electronic Arts develops and publishes video games and digital live services for global audiences across console, PC, and mobile platforms. Its portfolio combines internally owned franchises with heavily licensed sports properties, a model that turns annual releases, downloadable content, and recurring in-game spending into the industry's version of seasonal agriculture.

The company sits in the communication-services sector because its products are not just packaged games; they are persistent entertainment networks where players socialize, transact, watch, create, and return for ongoing content updates.

overview.title: Core franchises
paragraphs: EA Sports FC is the company's post-FIFA global football platform and remains one of EA's most visible sports properties. The franchise depends on official club, league, player, and broadcast-style licensing, while monetization is shaped by annual premium releases, Ultimate Team modes, subscriptions, and platform storefront distribution.

The Sims is EA's long-running life-simulation franchise, centered on creative play, identity expression, building tools, user-generated storytelling, and expansion-pack economics. It is a useful registry case because it blends proprietary game engines, controlled marketplaces, mod-adjacent communities, and a player base that often supplies the cultural energy while EA supplies the checkout flow.

overview.title: Registry relevance
paragraphs: EA's products map naturally against open and decentralized alternatives in game distribution, community hosting, modding infrastructure, identity systems, creator marketplaces, esports/community governance, and preservation. The company is especially relevant where closed platforms, license-controlled content, anti-cheat systems, and live-service shutdowns affect player autonomy.

For Free The World, EA should be researched as both a content company and a platform operator. The important question is not only whether there are open-source substitutes for individual games, but where users, creators, and communities can avoid dependence on proprietary accounts, rented digital goods, and server-side worlds that disappear when the spreadsheet says so.
